events.title: New Year's Day
events.summary: Federal, state and city holiday with heavy travel, nightlife and hospitality relevance after New Year's Eve.
research_notes: History: New Year's Day marks the first day of the Gregorian calendar and is one of the world's most widely recognized civic holidays. In New York, its meaning is inseparable from Times Square New Year's Eve: the city becomes a global stage for countdown culture, tourism, nightlife, public safety planning and the symbolic reset that follows the holiday season.
Who celebrates: Residents, visitors, hospitality, nightlife, families
Collaboration angle: Recovery brunch guides, hotel packages, alcohol-free reset content, public-space cleanup/community stories.

research_notes: History: Three Kings Day, or Dia de los Reyes, commemorates the visit of the Magi in Christian tradition and is especially important in Puerto Rican, Dominican, Mexican, Caribbean and broader Latino communities. In NYC it has long been celebrated through family gatherings, gift-giving, parades, museum programming and neighborhood events, making it a strong bridge between faith, childhood traditions and Latino cultural visibility.
Who celebrates: Families, Latino communities, schools, museums, neighborhood groups

events.title: Feast of San Gennaro

research_notes: History: The Feast of San Gennaro began in Little Italy in 1926 when Neapolitan immigrants honored Saint Januarius, the patron saint of Naples. Who celebrates: Italian American communities, tourists, food lovers, families
Collaboration angle: Vendor spotlights, cannoli/sausage guides, Little Italy history, restaurant partnerships.

Collaboration angle: Mexican food corridor guide, bakery and market features, flag explainers, mariachi/music listings.

events.title: Election Day
events.summary: Civic date with school-calendar relevance, polling-place logistics and public affairs content potential.
research_notes: History: Election Day is a civic anchor for voting, public schools and local political life. In NYC, even off-year elections can determine city and state offices, ballot questions and neighborhood-level power, making it a useful moment for nonpartisan voter education and civic literacy.
Who celebrates: Voters, families, civic organizations, students

events.title: TCS New York City Marathon
events.summary: The world's largest marathon runs through all five boroughs on the first Sunday in November, creating a rare citywide sports-and-community moment.
research_notes: History: The New York City Marathon began in 1970 with loops in Central Park before expanding into a five-borough race in 1976. Its significance is enormous: it turns the entire city into a course, combines elite sport with charity fundraising and neighborhood cheering, and gives every borough a visible role in one global event.
Who celebrates: Runners, families, global visitors, wellness brands, neighborhoods along course
Collaboration angle: Training series, cheering-zone maps, borough food guides, recovery partners, charity runner stories.

events.title: Veterans Day Parade
events.summary: The city's major Veterans Day commemoration and parade, with military, civic and family participation.
research_notes: History: Veterans Day began as Armistice Day, marking the end of World War I on November 11, 1918, and later expanded to honor all U.S. military veterans. New York's parade tradition makes the day a civic commemoration, connecting service, family memory, public gratitude and veteran support organizations.
Who celebrates: Veterans, families, civic groups, schools, nonprofits
Collaboration angle: Veteran nonprofit partnerships, service-member profiles, resource guides.
Content use: Prioritize respectful storytelling and practical route information.

events.title: Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade
events.summary: A nationally watched NYC holiday parade with balloons, performances, family travel and major brand visibility.
research_notes: History: Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade began in 1924 and became one of America's best-known holiday broadcasts. Its significance is both local and national: it turns Manhattan into a live performance corridor and signals the start of the holiday shopping season, family travel and brand storytelling.
Who celebrates: Families, tourists, brands, hotels, broadcasters
Collaboration angle: Hotel packages, family viewing guides, balloon inflation coverage, Thanksgiving dining partners.

events.title: Black Friday and Small Business Saturday
events.summary: A major retail and neighborhood-commerce weekend immediately after Thanksgiving.
research_notes: History: Black Friday is the Friday after Thanksgiving and has become a major U.S. retail shopping day. Small Business Saturday follows the next day and is widely used to push local shopping, neighborhood discovery and independent retail.
Who celebrates: Retailers, makers, restaurants, neighborhood BIDs, creators, gift-guide audiences
Collaboration angle: Gift guides, borough shopping maps, maker markets, local retail reels, restaurant/shop bundles.
Content use: Use this for collaboration packages with Queens, Brooklyn and Manhattan small businesses before holiday markets peak.

events.title: Cyber Monday and GivingTuesday
events.summary: Back-to-back digital commerce and nonprofit fundraising moments after Thanksgiving weekend.
research_notes: History: Cyber Monday is the Monday after Thanksgiving and is tied to online shopping. GivingTuesday follows on Tuesday and began as a global generosity movement, making it a natural day for nonprofits, mutual aid groups and mission-led brands.
Who celebrates: E-commerce brands, nonprofits, creators, donors, small businesses, community organizations
Collaboration angle: Affiliate campaigns, nonprofit donation drives, creator fundraising, holiday product drops, impact reports.
Content use: Separate the tone: Cyber Monday is offer-led; GivingTuesday should be mission-led and transparent about impact.

events.title: Holiday Markets, Hanukkah, Kwanzaa and Winter Programming

research_notes: History: NYC's holiday season layers many traditions at once: Christmas windows and trees, Hanukkah menorah lightings, Kwanzaa programming, winter markets, concerts, school breaks and tourism. Its significance is that New York becomes a stage for both religious observance and commercial spectacle.

research_notes: History: Times Square New Year's Eve grew from early 20th-century public celebrations around the New York Times building and the famous ball drop.
